The king of Babylon was kind to Jeremiah, yet the prophet must foretell the ruin of that kingdom. If your friends are God's enemies, you dare not speak peace to them. The destruction of Babylon is spoken of as being complete. Here is a word for the comfort of the Jews. They will return to their God first, then to their own land; the promise of their conversion and reformation makes way for the other promises. Their tears flow not from the sorrow of the world, as when they went into captivity, but from godly sorrow. They will seek after the Lord as their God, and have no more to do with idols. They will think of returning to their own country. This represents the return of poor souls to God. In true converts there are sincere desires to attain the end, and constant cares to keep in the way. Their present situation is lamented as very sad. The sins of professing Christians never will excuse those who rejoice in destroying them.

The desolation that will be brought upon Babylon is set forth in a variety of expressions. The cause of this destruction is the wrath of the Lord. Babylon will be wholly desolated, for she has sinned against the Lord. Sin makes men a mark for the arrows of God's judgments. The mercy promised to the Israel of God will not only accompany, but arise from the destruction of Babylon. These sheep will be gathered from the deserts and put again into good pasture. All who return to God and their duty will find satisfaction of soul in so doing. Deliverances out of trouble are comforts indeed, when they come with fruits of the forgiveness of sin.

The forces are mustered and empowered to destroy Babylon. Let them do what God demands, and they will bring to pass what he threatens. The pride of men's hearts sets God against them, and ripens them quickly for ruin. Babylon's pride must be her ruin; she has been proud against the Holy One of Israel; who can keep those up whom God will throw down?

It is Israel's comfort in distress that, though they are weak, their Redeemer is strong. This may be applied to believers who complain of the dominion of sin and corruption, and of their own weakness and manifold infirmities. Their Redeemer is able to keep what they commit to him; and sin will not have dominion over them. He will give them that rest which remains for the people of God. Also here is Babylon's sin, and their punishment. The sins are idolatry and persecution. He who will not save his people in their sins never will countenance the wickedness of his open enemies. The judgments of God for these sins will lay them waste. In the judgments denounced against prosperous Babylon, and the mercies promised to afflicted Israel, we learn to choose to suffer affliction with the people of God, rather than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season.
